title: Cosine Similarity
concept: Measuring the angle between two vectors, ignoring magnitude, as the default text-similarity metric.

exercises.prompt: Before we can compare the similarity of two pieces of text, we must first convert them into what data representation?
exercises.explain: In LLM applications, text is converted into list of numbers called embeddings (or vectors) before we can perform math or search operations on them.

cards.title: Measuring Meaning
cards.body: How do computers know that 'king' and 'queen' are related, or that two giant articles talk about the same topic? They turn words into arrows called vectors and measure the angle between them!

cards.title: What is Cosine Similarity?
cards.body: Cosine Similarity is a metric that measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors. It tells us how similar two vectors are in direction, ranging from -1 (opposite) to 1 (same direction), completely ignoring their size.

cards.title: The Flashlight Analogy
cards.body: Imagine two flashlight beams pointing in a dark room. Cosine similarity doesn't care how bright the beams are (their length); it only measures the angle between them to see if they are pointing in the same direction.

cards.title: Similarity in Action
cards.body: If you embed the sentence 'I love programming' and 'Coding is my favorite hobby', their vectors will point in almost the same direction in space, yielding a high cosine similarity score close to 0.99.

cards.title: Size Doesn't Matter
cards.body: Many beginners think longer documents will automatically seem less similar to short ones. But because cosine similarity ignores vector length (magnitude) and only looks at direction, a short summary can have a perfect similarity score with a long book!

cards.title: Visualizing the Angle
cards.body: Compare how Manhattan/Euclidean distance focuses on the space between endpoints, while Cosine Similarity only looks at the opening angle.

left.text: Measures straight-line distance. Highly affected by document length.

right.text: Measures the angle. Only cares about direction, ignoring length.

exercises.prompt: How does Cosine Similarity compare two vectors?

options.text: It measures the angle direction and ignores the magnitude (length).
It measures the exact physical distance between the endpoints of the vectors.
It adds the lengths of the two vectors together to find a total score.
It only works if the two vectors are exactly the same length.

exercises.explain: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which focuses entirely on their direction and ignores their length (magnitude).

exercises.prompt: True or False: A cosine similarity score of 1 means the two vectors point in the exact same direction.
exercises.explain: A cosine similarity of 1 means the vectors point in the exact same direction, meaning they are maximally similar.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how cosine similarity handles vector length.
exercises.sentence: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors and ignores their magnitude.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity ignores the magnitude (length) of the vectors and focuses solely on the angle between them.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations of cosine similarity was written by an AI trying to sound overly academic?

options.text: Cosine similarity is utilized heavily in LLMs. This metric evaluates the angular alignment between pair-wise vector representations, effectively neutralizing the impact of document length.
We use cosine similarity because we want to know if two sentences talk about the same thing, even if one sentence is much longer than the other.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ranging from -1 to 1.

exercises.explain: Option B uses natural, human-like phrasing. Option C is a classic template hallucination. Option A is an AI-generated paragraph that overly formalizes a simple query with robotic, repetitive structure ('This metric evaluates...').

exercises.prompt: If you compare a 10-page article about space travel with a 1-sentence summary of the same article using Cosine Similarity, what is the expected outcome?

options.text: It will be close to 0 because the lengths are so different
It will be very high (e.g., 0.95) because the directions are almost identical
It will be exactly 0 because length difference causes mathematical errors
It will be negative because the shorter text has less information

exercises.explain: Cosine similarity only looks at the angle between vectors. Because the long article and the short summary cover the exact same topics, their vectors point in the same direction in vector space, resulting in a high cosine similarity of 0.95 despite their very different lengths.
